title: Seward Derby: 78-year-old from Kasilof hits pay dirt
summary: At 1 p.m. Saturday, with only three hours left of the 50th Seward Silver Salmon Derby, Roy Hoekema tried staying calm at his Kasilof home. The leader of the derby he has entered just about every year since 1981 was poised to win $10,000, but there were still fishermen out in Resurrection Bay seeking something bigger. "I'm living on Tums, and my wife keeps checking the results," said Hoekema, a retired 78-year-old who spends his summers fishing out of one of his two boats. "I was still in the lead about an hour ago." By 4 p.m., the derby was over and once all the fish were weighed, Hoekema could put his Tums away and celebrate. His 17.42-pound silver salmon beat the second-place fish by less than a quarter of a pound. "We got two 17-pound fish and three 16-pounders (in this year's derby)," …

title: No, kayak anglers aren't crazy -- they're stable
summary: For the past six years, Christopher Mautino has had to convince people that fishing from a kayak is not as crazy as it might sound. Sure, kayaks are small, Alaska water is cold and the fish are big. The combination sounds wacky. But last week, as he reeled in a small halibut in Resurrection Bay while dangling his feet over the edge of his specially designed fishing kayak, the moment was incredible. Forget boats with loud motors creating big wakes. With little more than the sound of his line being reeled in, fishing from his kayak was a peaceful experience. Mautino owns Liquid Adventures, a Seward-based kayak tour company that also offers guided fishing trips from kayaks built especially for the sport. Most fishing is in water 20 feet to 200 feet deep for halibut, salmon or rockfish. "A mis…

title: Bear spray stops charging sow
summary: In the blink of an eye, a defensive grizzly bear sow was rolling like a freight train through the willows along Peters Creek. Then the brush was bathed in an orange-gray mist. And in that instant, hiker Carl Ramm saw the sow's eye go wide "and it was gone," he said. Neither Ramm nor his wife, Susan Alexander, clearly saw the bear leave. They heard it crashing through the brush as it beat a retreat with a cub trailing behind. "It sounded like a small cub," Ramm said. Big bruins See Alaska's bears -- safely. Anchorage area Denali Park Inside Passage Arctic Alaska Katmai and Kodiak Bear too big to believe Bear safety tips The couple hadn't known until almost the last moment that they had somehow walked between a grizzly sow and her cub along the Peters Creek Trail, eight or nine miles into Ch…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Winter kings relieve 'graydoms' SILVER FLASH: Not-so-hot fishing better than a gray day stuck in city. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: December 25, 2005 Last Modified: December 25, 2005 at 04:50 AM When the Winter Wonderland that has been Alaska for hundreds of years becomes slushy, muddy and gray like a far-north Seattle suburb, what's an outdoors man or woman to do? Go fishing, of course. That's one Puget Sound solution to the graydoms, a mood disorder brought on by gray skies meeting a gray landscape. A segment of the Northwest population has long treated this ailment by heading for a boat, firing up the motor, grabbing some rods and looking for feeder king salmon. Over the past decade, as the Alaska climate has warmed, more and more Alaskans have discovered that what wo…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Computer-generated McKinley replica may help prepare climbers By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: December 25, 2005 Last Modified: December 30, 2005 at 03:01 AM Harry Hunt examined the Cassin Ridge route, labeled "F" and marked by yellow string on a model of Mount McKinley on display at the U.S. Geological Survey office at Alaska Pacific University. He shook his head and grinned. The string snaked almost vertically up the south side of the three-dimensional rendering of the 20,320-foot mountain, testament to its reputation as perhaps the most technical climbing route to the summit. "I don't know," said Hunt, chairman of the Alaska section of the American Alpine Club, who first climbed McKinley in 1990 by ascending Cassin Ridge. "That was my first climbing trip in Alaska. It…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Valley streams ripe for king fishing (6-23-2005) KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: June 23, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:32 AM MONTANA CREEK -- A woman's shriek broke the silence Monday afternoon, startling angler Mike Paesani and causing him to look up from the tangled reel he was tending. Until that moment, there had been little to hear but Montana Creek's current folding into the silty Susitna River. The alder jungles had fallen quiet as a scattered bunch of anglers stood at the waters' edge, some drifting Corkies or Spin-N-Glos while others listlessly cast Vibrax spinners or Pixee spoons. Then came the shriek, followed by delighted laughter and the unmistakable splashing sounds of a big fish wallowing in the current. "Sounds like somebody's got a king," said Paesnai, o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Dollies gain reputation as a fine sportfish (8-15-2003) ACCESSIBLE: Once they were considered trash fish and carried a bounty. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: August 15, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:27 AM COOPER LANDING -- I wanted pictures Tuesday afternoon of anglers catching and releasing big Dolly Varden but couldn't find anyone on the creek to photograph. So I worked my way both upstream and down, catching fish up to 2 feet long -- and some perhaps longer. I had the place to myself. Times like this make me wish I could paint. Maybe then I could capture the little stream's way of collecting the Kenai Mountains in its pools and making the spawning red salmon -- all scarlet, green-headed and hook-nosed -- appear to float over alpine valleys. Maybe then I could…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Trophy-class grayling lurk in Susitna Valley streams (7-4-2003) NICE DIGS: The grayling's beauty is often surpassed only by the watersheds it inhabits. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 4, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:26 AM My first cast brought a strike -- nothing dramatic, just a light pluck -- but I tried to set the hook too quickly and missed the fish. On the second drift, as my fly tumbled out of the swift water and into the heart of the pool, I hooked something heavy. My light fly rod arched sharply, and deep down near the bottom something flashed like a knife blade. There were no spectacular leaps or sizzling runs. Instead, the fish fought to hold its place in the pool, bulldogging stubbornly and shaking its head. I knew, even before I got a good look …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tell the boss you've got King fever (5-23-2003) EARLY OUTBREAK: Fish are in, so use that sick leave to get out of office and on the water. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: May 23, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:25 AM Burma Landing -- The Little Susitna River was quiet, not another boat to be heard or angler to be seen, when Andy Couch pulled up to a big pool and cut his riverboat engine. The water was unusually low for May 1 and crystal-clear -- likely the result of dry spring weather and limited runoff from a light winter snowpack. Couch picked up his rod, clipped a small spinner onto the swivel and, on a fisherman's hunch, cocked his rod back and sent the spinner flying across the pool. For a moment there was nothing to see but beams of afternoon sunlight softene…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Steelhead are last fix for fanatical fisherman (9-13-2002) THRILLING: Hooking into a sea-run rainbow is as good as it gets. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: September 13, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:25 AM September is a fickle month, clear and frosty one morning, wet and cold the next. As the season works through this bipolar phase, caught between brilliant fall color and the raw misery of autumn rains, it is tempting for anglers to hang up their tackle and call it a summer. Yet for a small subgroup -- a stubborn bunch of hard-core anglers -- this final hour is the season's best. Each September, these folks trade in their lightweight, breathable GoreTex waders of summer for thick, warm neoprenes. They give up T-shirts for polypro long johns, and sunscreen for fi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Anchorage offers bevy of settings to hook fish (8-23-2002) AN URBAN BLISS: Anglers have options in Alaska's biggest city. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: August 23, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:24 AM A power line cut across the mountain's shoulder, providing clear passage to the stream where Luke Frazier of Logan, Utah, and I would fish. The distance from the gravel road where we'd parked to the canyon bottom was short -- perhaps 300 yards. To get there, we followed a trail through chest-high grass. The moose tracks in the trail impressed Frazier, who was used to the smaller prints left by mule deer in his home state. He wondered if bears were around too. I assured him they were. We hiked down the trail until the grass gave way to a thick growth of ferns at the …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Toothy predators strike hard, then tire (6-4-2004) FISHING REPORT: Pike may be unwanted by state biologists, but many anglers love to stalk them. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: June 4, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:28 AM NANCY LAKE STATE RECREATION AREA -- The bass popper plopped into the muskeg pond between shoreline and a clump of lily pads. For a moment it rested there, floating on the coffee-colored water like a little green-and-black frog. Just then, a cloud blocked the sun, and a robin that had been singing its heart out fell silent. A deathly hush swept over the pond. Then it happened. Abruptly as a jack-in-the box, a creature burst from the waters with a violent splash. Resembling a cross between an alligator and a snake, it grabbed the popper in its jaw…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Russian teeming with reds (6-18-2004) PLETHORA: Anglers are catching their limits; the sanctuary opens 6 a.m. today. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: June 18, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:28 AM RUSSIAN RIVER -- The air here Tuesday evening was electric. A crisp wind surged, anglers shouted as fishing lines popped and expensive rods broke, and salmon could be seen splashing and leaping just about everywhere. Opening day of fishing on the Russian River had begun at 12:01 a.m. and by all accounts -- including the state's report that afternoon of a whopping 8,763 fresh red salmon through the weir upstream -- the early-season red run was off to one of its strongest starts in recent memory. Outside the Russian River Ferry office, anglers carrying fish filed by like tri…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: A dry line never spins a good tale (5-24-2002) LET THE YARNS BEGIN: Legends of spring await those who venture to the Kenai and beyond. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: May 24, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:22 AM A morning spent trolling for king salmon near Ninilchik last Sunday left Ron Paye amazed at the way things in life sometimes come together. For Paye, an automation technician for the Anchorage School District, the trip produced not only salmon -- and a limit of halibut on the side -- but an unlikely, well, togetherness with another angler, one Lee Waronker of South Miami. Paye and Waronker, who was up from Florida to conduct an appraisal seminar in Anchorage earlier in the week, were among six anglers signed up for a day of fishing in Cook Inlet with Ninil…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Sometimes getting there is half the fun of fishing trip (8-6-2004) OFF THE ROAD: Cycling, hiking and horseback riding are all pleasurable options. KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 6, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:29 AM CRESCENT LAKE -- I had climbed perhaps 1,000 feet over 6 1 /2 miles, a gradual ascent by the standards of experienced mountain bikers. Yet somehow my trek over Crescent Creek Trail, 98 highway miles south of Anchorage, had seemed longer and more grueling under Tuesday's hot sun. I thought of the many places the trail had appeared to shoot straight up, and of the hairpin switchbacks dangerously broken by spruce roots and tire-jolting rocks. It didn't help that I carried a heavy pack full of fishing gear, or that my biking skills were barely sufficient …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Russian River reds are in, mates (6-21-2002) PACKED WITH ANGLERS: But fishermen are limiting quickly, so the wait is proving worthwhile. By Ken Marsh Daily News Reporter Published: June 21, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:23 AM So how about those red salmon? Everyone knew they were coming, but the huge numbers of fresh sockeyes that stacked up last weekend at the confluence of the Russian and Kenai rivers took even fishery managers by surprise. "We generally have 5,000 to 10,000 fish in the fishery when the sanctuary opens up," said Kenai sport fishery biologist Larry Marsh. "We don't generally have 30,000 to 40,000 available as we did this time." Many anglers who filed down the cottonwood-lined Russian River trails for opening day on Sunday were reportedly catching three fish-…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Missing kings are a mystery (6-14-2002) NEVER FEAR: Kenai River run confusing, but Peninsula has other options. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: June 14, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:22 AM Mystery is one of the spices of life. A little can be titillating; too much can lead to heartburn. Right now a lack of early-run king salmon in the Kenai River has sportfish biologists scratching their heads and reaching for antacids. The question: Where are the fish? The answer: Nobody knows. As of Tuesday, state sonar counts revealed only 3,136 kings had entered the river. By the same time last year, more than twice that many -- 6,809 -- had arrived. Some speculate the salmon have fallen victim to a natural disaster, like the redd-scouring floods of 1995. Or perhaps the world…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Silvers swarm waters (8-9-2002) GET THE NET: They're running wild all over Southcentral Alaska. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: August 9, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:24 AM They're aggressive, hard fighting and -- best of all -- they're back. Silvers are the last, best salmon of our summer, living proof that Southcentral's August clouds do indeed have precious linings. Right now, the spunky game fish are flooding into area waters from Resurrection Bay outside of Seward to Clear Creek in the Susitna Valley. "Cottonwood Creek's just full of silvers right now," said Dave Rutz, a state sportfish biologist in Palmer. "And Eklutna (tail race) is phenomenal." That's just for starters. In the Susitna Valley, dime-bright fish have arrived in the Parks Highway streams fro…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Time to go after the fish with fangs (6-6-2003) GET AFTER 'EM: Pike fishing is at its peak. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: June 6, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:25 AM Alexander Lake -- We caught them all day long, from 9 a.m. Sunday when the plane dropped us off until pickup time at 3:30 that afternoon. The fish struck like sharks, charging through sedges -- and sometimes leaping out of the water -- to hammer bass poppers, deer-hair mice and red-and-yellow rabbit-hide streamers. The fish were northern pike, Alexander Lake's species du jour ever since these voracious predators, illegally stocked in the Susitna Valley roughly 50 years ago, arrived here and gobbled up native stocks of rainbow trout and grayling. Most of the pike Anchorage angler Mark Kufel and I ca…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Taking a chance can pay off big (6-13-2003) RAINBOWS: Lucille Lake fly-rodder tires, sets up a bobber and shrimp, and hits. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: June 13, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:25 AM WASILLA -- On a recent Sunday afternoon, as the sun broke from the clouds and glared brilliantly through the open door of Three Rivers Fly and Tackle, angler Chris Watchus appeared in the entryway and asked, "Do you guys have a tape measure?" Heads turned as Tom Cloninger, the man behind the counter, said sure. Then, as Watchus stepped out of the sunlight and into the room, Cloninger's eyes widened ever so slightly as he added, "We've got a scale too." Watchus hoisted two rainbow trout -- with a cumulative weight of nearly 8 pounds -- attached to a metal chain-and-c…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cold kings (6-27-2003) When water heats up, fish keep mouths shut By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: June 27, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:26 AM SUSITNA LANDING -- The time was 6:15 a.m. and Susitna Valley river guide Ron Wilson was already being a stubborn old cuss. Arms folded across his chest, he faced his four clients and flatly refused to take them fishing for king salmon on the Deshka River. Yes, Wilson knew the Deshka was boasting an extraordinary run of kings -- more than 28,000 and counting. And yes, he was quite aware the state had responded by raising bag limits to two fish per angler. But no, anybody fishing with Wilson on Saturday wasn't going to cast into the Deshka. For a moment, Wilson and his clients fell silent. Across from the small dock at the j…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Switch targets (8-29-2003) Salmon fishing slow, but Dollies, rainbows ready to fight anglers By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: August 29, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:27 AM STERLING -- At first it appeared the woman had drowned, because all you could see was her straw-colored hair above the water and a middle-aged man leaning as far as he dared over the side of the boat, tugging at her collar. But then the current spun her around as she and the boat drifted down the center of the Kenai River, and you could see her blue eyes rolling and her mouth open as she fought to breathe. Her face was white as the clouds. Mark Kufel and Doug Chace, both of Anchorage, were anchored up fishing for silver salmon last Saturday as the scene unfolded perhaps 30 yards away. Kufel, a …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Stuck in town? Trout, Dolly fishing's fine in streams (7-11-2003) HANDY: Campbell, Ship hold plenty of pleasant surprises. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 11, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:26 AM SHIP CREEK -- Well upstream from Ship Creek's salmon-fishing mobs and Cook Inlet's sticky mud flats, out of sight of the warehouses and oily industrial yards between Fifth Avenue and Post Road, flows an unlikely anglers oasis as fertile and feral as any stream this side of the Susitna Valley. Within a few strides downstream of the creek's Reeve Boulevard crossing, the concrete and chain-link fences of the city's port district dissolve into willow thickets and cottonwood groves. Here the stream purls as sweetly as it ever has and, behind schools of brick-red king salm…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: The rockfish files (7-18-2003) 32 species of the tasty fighters share Sound By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 18, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:26 AM WHITTIER -- A blue heron flew past our islet campsite each evening, and we were visited in the mornings by rufous hummingbirds that hovered around buoys. Wildflowers topped rocky beaches in splashes of purple and white, and salmonberries grew sweet in the shade of hemlock forests. Most colorful and exotic of all, though, were the cutthroat trout we hooked in a stream at the head of the bay, and nothing tasted better than the rockfish we caught offshore on light fly rods. Thanks to the Anton Anderson Memorial Tunnel, which opened three years ago to link Whittier to Southcentral's highway system, Prince William Sou…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cooler days mean trout in Valley lakes are biting (8-22-2003) FISHING REPORT By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: August 22, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:27 AM It seemed last weekend that the wet days of August had finally arrived. The Yentna River and Lake Creek were flooding, and the streets of Anchorage were awash with late-summer rain. Many Susitna Valley silver salmon anglers came home skunked, grousing about high stream levels and this season's weak runs. And yet, for a savvy few familiar with the notoriously fickle weather that comes as summer edges into fall, there were some angling bright spots. In fact, there were -- and are -- dozens of them. In some ways, last weekend's rainy Saturday was a nice break, perfect for tying flies or repairing reels, visiting …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: State biologist peeks into the life of Valley's rainbow trout (9-5-2003) STUDY: Scientist wants to know what effects fall fishery's popularity will have. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 5, 2003 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:27 AM PALMER -- There I was, wading a lovely Susitna Valley stream, ducking ferns and alders that hung over the banks, and catching rainbow trout big enough to bend my light rod like a broom straw. All I had to do, it seemed, was drop my fly into the riffles or along a dark run formed by a fallen tree and -- BAM! -- I was into another leaping, thick-shouldered trout. The early September air was spiced with the smell of ripe berries and each fish seemed a living gem, electrically charged and hand-painted by an artful creator. In the back o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Beads don't fly with some fishermen (7-9-2005) SPAWNING CONTROVERSY: Traditional fly-fishers eschew use of attractors despite effectiveness. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 9, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:32 AM The fish were there Tuesday, just as I expected they would be. Under a hot sun glaring from the cloudless Susitna Valley sky, you could see their scarlet shapes glowing near the bottom of deep pools. Those fish, of course, were spawning king salmon. Happy as I was to see them, salmon weren't what I sought. Like many Southcentral Alaska anglers who will soon find themselves caught between the closure of the Parks Highway king salmon fisheries on July 13 and the second run of Russian River reds, I was interested in fish of another color. Before long, I…

summary: Beads don't fly with some fishermen (7-9-2005) SPAWNING CONTROVERSY: Traditional fly-fishers eschew use of attractors despite effectiveness. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 9, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:32 AM The fish were there Tuesday, just as I expected they would be. Under a hot sun glaring from the cloudless Susitna Valley sky, you could see their scarlet shapes glowing near the bottom of deep pools. Those fish, of course, were spawning king salmon. Happy as I was to see them, salmon weren't what I sought. Like many Southcentral Alaska anglers who will soon find themselves caught between the closure of the Parks Highway king salmon fisheries on July 13 and the second run of Russian River reds, I was interested in fish of another color. Before long, I…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Ideal places to get kids hooked on fishing (7-23-2004) SPECIAL SETTINGS: Alaska has abundance of areas that are perfect learning environments. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 23, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:29 AM LOWER SUMMIT LAKE -- They were little fish for little girls, native goldenfin Dolly Varden mostly -- a diminutive subspecies found in the brooks and high-country tarns of the Chugach and Kenai mountains. A few small rainbow trout were among them. By little I mean six inches long with "trophies" topping 10 inches. But what the fish lacked in size they made up for in abundance, pluck and a willingness to strike No. 12 dry flies of all types. Besides, as far as Natalie, 11, and sister Stephanie, 8, were concerned Tuesday evening, size was not the poi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Hot weather, no rain can sink fish populations (8-13-2004) TROUT, CHAR, SALMON: 70-degree waters cause die-offs, push fish to cool stream mouths. KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 13, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:29 AM TALKEETNA -- The temperature was 80 degrees Saturday afternoon when I arrived at one of my favorite Susitna Valley trout lakes. That's too hot for fishing, but I walked down the trail anyway for a look at the water. I was in for a surprise. Two women were swimming along the drop-off where I normally launch my float tube. "How's the water?" I asked. Startled, the women looked up, then one answered tentatively, "It's wonderful." "Hmm. See any fish down there?" "No. No fish. Just Esther here," she said, nodding toward her companion. The women, treading w…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Winding down (8-27-2004) Good halibut left to be caught if you get fishing soon KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 27, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:30 AM WHITTIER -- Forty fathoms beneath the charter boat T. Rex on Sunday, in the murky depths of Montague Strait, something heavy had grabbed Chris McDonald's baited hook. And now the short, stubby saltwater rod McDonald held seemed possessed. It bucked and wrenched and bent perilously over the gunwale. The veins in McDonald's forearms swelled, and beads of sweat formed over his brow. Framed by the cabin door, Riptide Charters skipper Jay Jenkins smiled broadly, his lower lip fat with a double pinch of snuff. For five years now the 31-year-old former Coloradan has made his living in Whittier, taking anglers fishing on th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Mat-Su lakes worth a cast (5-31-2002) BRING BUG JUICE: With a little patience, rainbows will play along. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: May 31, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:22 AM You'll have to deal with mosquitoes if you decide to fish the lakes of the Matanuska and Susitna valleys right now. The little bloodsuckers were plenty active Sunday evening, but so were the rainbow trout. The shallows swarmed with fish as I stood on the shore of one of my favorite Palmer-area lakes, rubbing bug dope on my ears and planning my strategy. The trout that splashed within inches of the shore weren't feeding, they were spawning -- or at least going through the motions. For successful spawning, trout require running water to oxygenate their eggs. Fish stocked by the state in …

title: Craig Medred: Going hiking? Don't forget duct tape (8-7-2005)
summary: TWENTYMILE RIVER -- By the time we returned empty-handed from the futile search for the missing paddle shaft, Jim Jager had reason to smile. Standing on a gravel bar a long day's hike, maybe two, from the Seward Highway, he held in his hands the latest in Alaska paddling exotica: A carbon-fiber, alder-stick, bent-shaft kayak paddle. He had only one question for Jacques Boutet and myself: Did the jury-rigged paddle need more duct tape? Boutet, who'd precipitated this invention by losing half of the two-piece shaft from his four-piece paddle on a hike over Winner Creek Trail from Girdwood, said that it did. By now, hours had been lost scouring a heavily used bear trail -- the only good route down from Winner Creek Pass -- through some of the thickest cover around looking for a shaft we didn'…

title: Forget your fat dog; Iams wants to talk to you (2-13-2005)
summary: Breathes there a yuppie puppy not spoiled rotten already? If so, help is on the way from the Iams Company, a division of Procter & Gamble. The marketing geniuses who convinced Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race champ Rick Swenson to wear that horrid, Pepto-Bismol pink jumpsuit are rolling out a line of pup-dilly-icious "canine condiments'' to -- as they put it -- "spoil your dog healthy.'' What, you ask, are "canine condiments''? Well, to be honest, I haven't tasted them. But if the marketing is to be believed, we're talking bacon grease and gravy. If, of course, bacon grease and gravy were expensive. "The sauces,'' according to The New York Times, "come in plastic bottles in two sizes, 11 ounces and 16.5 ounces, and in three flavors, Country Style Chicken, Roasted Beef and Sizzlin' Bacon. The 1…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Don't let one mistake on water cascade into more (10-23-2005) CRAIG MEDRED Outdoors Published: October 23, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:20 AM SKILAK LAKE -- Less than a mile from the beach with the bow of the Klepper kayak plunging into the front of the waves and darkness closing in, the decision to gamble on a late-night crossing of this notoriously wind-tossed lake looked frighteningly bad. Just tens of minutes before, the night had offered such promise. Winds that had been blowing strong out of the east for two days appeared to be easing. Daylight was, admittedly, fading fast, but a bright moon was rising. So we broke camp, slammed gear as fast as possible into the two-man kayak, slipped into dry suits and personal flotation devices, grabbed the paddles and …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Seward salmon are biting (7-16-2004) SILVERS: Larger than normal fish are being caught in Resurrection Bay. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 16, 2004 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:28 AM SEWARD -- Angler Cheryl Myers of Apple Valley, Calif., stepped aboard the charter boat Glacier Breeze on Sunday morning quietly wondering what the day might bring. Retired and enjoying her first trip to Alaska, she had never in her life caught a silver salmon. A few minutes spent fishing the salty green swells of Resurrection Bay would quickly change that. About an hour's run from the Seward small-boat harbor, Breeze skipper Chad Washburn, a slight and sprightly 21-year-old with curly red hair and seemingly boundless energy, cut the engine outside Agnes Cove. Lit by a low morning s…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Lazy days are sweet (9-6-2002) WINDING DOWN: Crowds are gone and the fishing is peaceful. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: September 6, 2002 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:24 AM Something heavy is afoot in Southcentral Alaska. Signs are everywhere. Campgrounds from the Susitna Valley to Anchor Point have grown quiet and nearly empty as the silver-salmon run, the last best fish of the season, winds down. And the boardwalks of Seward, Homer and Whittier -- bustling in July -- are getting lonelier by the day as saltwater charter fleets wrap up another summer. Yes, folks, read it and weep: The glory days of the 2002 fishing season seem to be over. Or are they? Conversations with Alaska biologists and hardcore anglers this week indicate otherwise. title: Tides lift silvers into Bird Creek (8-4-2005)
summary: BIRD CREEK -- The fishing got crazy around 4:30 Monday afternoon, as the incoming tide began its sweep up Turnagain Arm. Where the green-tinted estuary had earlier slithered along the bottom of a slick mud gut, it now flowed deep and was on the way to filling its banks. Salmon rolled everywhere. And for the handful of anglers who braved occasional torrents of blowing, in-your-face rain, the action at this popular Seward Highway fishery 25 miles south of Anchorage was excellent for hard-running, sea-bright silvers. Among those anglers was visiting East Coast fisherman Govinddass Goleman. Dragging a pair of still-flopping silvers, Goleman paused midway across the newly built skywalk over Bird Creek. "I went to the bait shop this morning and they told me the silvers weren't in here yet -- whi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Cyclist happy to be alive after violent hit-and-run by SUV Published: December 25, 2005 Last Modified: December 25, 2005 at 04:50 AM Six hours of cyclist Chris Turner's life are missing. From the time Turner left his girlfriend at The Middle Way Cafe in Midtown shortly after midnight on Dec. 8 until he recognized he was in a hospital bed hours later, his life is a blank. Over those missing six hours, the 23-year-old Spenard resident experienced the worst of human nature -- and the best. Anchorage police say Turner was crossing the intersection of Minnesota and Benson boulevards on his bike when he was hit by a sport utility vehicle that ran a red light. The collision was so violent Turner and the bike were physically thrown 20 to 30 feet. Turner was knocked unconscious. The d…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Bundle up and hop on a snowmachine to find winter fishing (11-25-2005) By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: November 25, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 05:53 AM You'll need a snowmachine for this fishing trip. Most anglers this time of year reach the lake from Susitna Landing, a privately owned parking area off the Parks Highway north of Willow. Operators Ron and Marilyn Wilson keep the place plowed, and you pay them a few bucks to leave your vehicle and snowmachine trailer. Then you load your sled with the basics -- tackle, rods, bait, auger, a thermos full of something steaming hot -- rev up your machine, and blast off into the wilderness. Getting there is half the fun. The other half is the rush you feel catching dozens of northern pike (the fish of choice in this larg…
